lesson_contract.objective: Practice adaptable responses for price, timing, trust, competition, and decision-maker concerns.
objective_points: Select a script pattern that matches the objection
Adapt wording without changing its intent
Know when to pause and seek guidance
lesson_contract.transition_spoken_text: Take the Objection Scripts Playbook checkpoint next. Then move into Trust, Complexity, and Frequently Asked Questions.

forbidden_language_removals.exact_source_language: If I can guarantee your net
forbidden_language_removals.replacement_rule: Ask which written option the seller wants reviewed. Never guarantee proceeds.

forbidden_language_removals.exact_source_language: you get a clean, certain outcome without games
forbidden_language_removals.replacement_rule: Describe the review process without promising an outcome.

forbidden_language_removals.exact_source_language: your price, timeline, and certainty don't change
forbidden_language_removals.replacement_rule: State that final terms depend on the written agreement and review.

forbidden_language_removals.exact_source_language: I can bring the loan current, stop the sale, and take over payments so you can breathe again and rebuild your credit
forbidden_language_removals.replacement_rule: Pause, avoid legal or credit promises, and route the facts for qualified review.

forbidden_language_removals.exact_source_language: I can close, fund you, and let you stay
forbidden_language_removals.replacement_rule: Discuss occupancy only as a written option subject to approval.

scenes.title: Use patterns, not promises
scenes.spoken_text: An objection is not a cue to talk faster. It is a cue to find out what the seller is protecting. Use these responses as patterns. Adapt the words to the facts, and do not promise a price, date, approval, legal result, or financial result that has not been confirmed in writing.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea standing in a medium shot
visual_plan.editor_note: Open with a direct, grounded delivery.

scenes.title: Price concern
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: That number is too low. Response: I hear you. What number were you expecting, and what does that number need to cover? Let them answer. Then ask whether price is the only concern or whether timing, repairs, and fees matter too. If a different structure may fit, collect the facts and have it reviewed before you describe it as available.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea seated across from an empty chair
visual_plan.editor_note: Optional seller dialogue can appear as simple captions. Keep figures off screen.

scenes.title: Need to think
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: I need to think about it. Response: Of course. What part feels unresolved right now? It may be the price, the timing, another person who needs to weigh in, or a question we have not answered. Find the missing piece, agree on the next action, and give the seller room to decide.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea in a calm seated medium shot
visual_plan.editor_note: Hold a short pause after the seller line.

scenes.title: Comparing another offer
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: I have another offer. Response: That makes sense. What matters most as you compare them? Ask about the written price, deductions, conditions, timing, and who is responsible for each cost. Do not attack the other offer. Help the seller compare the documents and the tradeoffs they can verify.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea beside two blank document folders
visual_plan.editor_note: Optional cutaway compares written terms without logos or invented numbers.

scenes.title: Considering a traditional sale
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: I may put it on the market. Response: That may be a good fit. What would need to be true for that path to work for you? Ask about preparation, access, timing, fees, and the seller's tolerance for uncertainty. If they want professional advice outside your scope, say so and let them get it.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea standing beside a simple house outline
visual_plan.editor_note: Do not name a provider or professional title.

scenes.title: Trust and proof
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: How do I know this is legitimate? Response: You should verify who you are dealing with. I can share the company information and written documents available for this conversation. Take the time you need to review them. If you have a specific concern, tell me what it is so I can answer it or find the right answer.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea with a closed document folder
visual_plan.editor_note: Keep all documents generic and readable only as blank forms.

scenes.title: Another decision maker
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: I need to talk to someone else. Response: That makes sense. What will they want to understand before the conversation can move forward? If it helps, we can schedule a time when everyone can hear the same information and ask questions. Do not try to work around a person whose consent matters.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea at one side of a small round table
visual_plan.editor_note: Optional cutaway shows two empty seats joining the discussion.

scenes.title: Timing concern
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: I cannot move that fast. Response: What needs to happen before a move is workable? Ask about housing, belongings, family coordination, and any outside deadline. Record the facts. Discuss only timing that the written process can support, and confirm the final date in the agreement.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea seated with a blank calendar card
visual_plan.editor_note: No dates or time promises on screen.

scenes.title: Fees and repairs
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: What will I have to pay or fix? Response: Let's separate what is known from what still needs review. We can go through the written terms, the condition information, and the costs assigned to each side. If a cost is not confirmed, do not guess. Mark the question and get a written answer.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea beside a two-column blank worksheet
visual_plan.editor_note: Use labels such as confirmed and needs review. No amounts.

scenes.title: Legal, loan, or credit pressure
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: I am behind and I need this stopped. Response: I am sorry you are dealing with that. I need to be careful not to promise a legal, loan, or credit result. Let me collect the deadline, the notices you received, and the contact information on them. We can route the facts for qualified review. You should also seek independent advice when the situation calls for it.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea in a steady close medium shot
visual_plan.editor_note: No foreclosure countdown, legal seal, or rescue imagery.

scenes.title: Staying after the transaction
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: I need time in the property afterward. Response: Thank you for saying that now. Any stay after the transaction would need a written occupancy agreement and approval before anyone relies on it. Tell me how much flexibility you need and why. I will document the request so the available options can be reviewed.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea standing with a house key prop resting on a table
visual_plan.editor_note: Do not imply the request has been approved.

scenes.title: Know when to pause
scenes.spoken_text: Pause when the question crosses into legal advice, credit advice, tax advice, lending, title, a special contract term, or an exception to the current process. Write down the exact question. Tell the seller you are getting it reviewed. Then follow through with a confirmed answer.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea at a desk writing one question
visual_plan.editor_note: Use a deliberate pause before the final sentence.

scenes.title: Practice the pattern
scenes.spoken_text: For each objection, practice the same sequence. Acknowledge the concern. Ask one question that reveals what sits underneath it. Separate confirmed facts from assumptions. Offer only a next step that the current process supports. Then check that the seller understands what happens next.

visual_plan.editor_note: Keep the step card generic and free of provider branding.

scenes.title: Continue to the checkpoint
scenes.spoken_text: Take the Objection Scripts Playbook checkpoint next. Then move into Trust, Complexity, and Frequently Asked Questions.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea centered in a medium shot

edit_operations.source_problem: Direct outcome guarantees are spread across multiple taught comeback beats. A full recut preserves the objection categories and practice method while replacing promises with conditional discovery and review language.
